title: Dog Abdominal Heaving While Breathing: Emergency Guide and Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog struggling to breathe? Learn why abdominal heaving is a high-priority emergency and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Abdominal heaving is a life-threatening medical emergency indicating your dog is struggling to breathe; you must transport them to an emergency veterinarian immediately. This condition leads to rapid oxygen deprivation and cannot wait for home monitoring.

What does it mean if my dog is experiencing abdominal heaving?

Abdominal heaving occurs when a dog uses their stomach muscles to assist with the breathing process. In a healthy dog, breathing should be quiet and require minimal effort from the chest. When you see the belly or sides moving significantly with every breath, it is a sign that the dog is working much harder than normal to pull air into the lungs or push it out. This is often referred to as labored breathing or dyspnea.

Is abdominal heaving in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-urgency medical emergency that requires immediate intervention.
  • Respiratory distress can lead to rapid oxygen deprivation and life-threatening complications.
  • Do not wait to see if the condition improves at home.
  • Transport your dog immediately to the nearest 24-hour emergency hospital if you notice heaving, neck stretching, or blue, purple, or pale gums.

How does a video or photo of my dog’s breathing help the veterinary team?

  • If safe, record a 10-second video of the breathing before you leave to provide an invaluable diagnostic tool.
  • A video helps identify issues that may be masked by a temporary adrenaline surge when the dog arrives at the clinic.
  • Visual evidence helps the vet make a faster, more accurate diagnosis and determine the level of immediate intervention required.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in dogs manifests as increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by changes in respiratory pattern and postural changes such as standing with elbows abducted and back arched or high on the rear haunches with head and neck extended. Labored, open-mouth breathing and cyanosis indicate significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. Pleural space disease causes asynchronous breathing where the chest expands on inspiration as the abdomen is pulled inward, then the chest moves inward on expiration as the abdomen expands. Lung parenchymal disease causes quiet, smooth breathing, with the chest and abdominal wall moving in the same direction. In cases of gastric dilatation-volvulus (GDV), the expanding stomach may compress the thoracic cavity and inhibit diaphragmatic movement, leading to respiratory distress. Thoracic auscultation can help differentiate between pleural space and parenchymal disease. Stabilizing the animal is paramount; radiographs should be performed after stabilization to avoid decompensation.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: What are common causes of abdominal breathing in dogs?
faqs.answer: Potential causes include congestive heart failure, pneumonia, allergic reactions, asthma, chest trauma, or an obstruction in the airway.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my dog to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Avoid stressing the dog, as excitement or heat increases their need for oxygen, which they are already struggling to get.

faqs.question: Can panting be confused with abdominal heaving?
faqs.answer: Panting is usually fast and shallow with the tongue out. Abdominal heaving involves deep, forceful movements of the belly and is often a sign of true distress rather than just being hot.
